data.title: Innate: Lacutamab Treatment Program Moves Forward With Potential Expansion
data.description:
Innate Pharma (IPHA) had recently received Breakthrough Therapy Designation [BTD] for its anti-KIR3DL2 antibody candidate known as lacutamab. This particular antibody is being developed for the treatment of adult patients with relapsed or refractory Sezary Syndrome [r/r SS]. This program is highly ideal, and the company was already able to report positive results from its open-label phase 2 study using this drug as a monotherapy to treat these patients with r/r SS. Besides the BTD it just received from the FDA today on February 19th 2025, it has already received two other designations from it, which are Fast Track Designation [FTD] from the FDA in 2019 and then PRIME Designation from the European Medicines Agency [EMA] in 2020. Why should investors care about this program now though, with phase 2 data already being reported? That's because the company already noted itself that it is actively seeking a partner in order to advance this program of targeting CTCL patients into a phase 3 study.

Thus, it is possible that such a partnership or collaboration agreement could come at any moment. Secondly, because this is a versatile drug in that it can target another form of Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma [NHL], which is Peripheral T-cell Lymphoma [PTCL]. The company believes that it is able to release data from an ongoing phase 2 study using monotherapy lacutamab to treat these specific cancer patients in 2025. The beauty of this company is that it has other candidates that it is adding into its pipeline to diversify itself. This would be the ability to add a few antibody drug conjugates [ADC] into the mix. Specifically, there is one ADC known as IPH4502 being explored in a phase 1 study to target patients with Nectin-4 tumors. The importance of this is that the company believes that it is going to be able to release preliminary safety and efficacy data from this early-stage trial in 2026.

Lacutamab Is A Proven Asset With Another Shot On Goal

As I have stated directly above at the beginning of this article, the company received Breakthrough Therapy Designation [BTD] from the FDA of lacutamab for the treatment of patients with relapsed/refractory Sezary Syndrome. The company was able to complete an open-label phase 2 TELLOMAK study for this program, showing that patients were able to achieve a pretty good response rate. Especially, considering that these were patients who had previously failed two prior systemic therapies including mogamulizumab [marketed as POTELIGEO by Kyowa Kirin]. Before going over this mid-stage trial data, plus any catalysts that are to be released from this specific program, it is first important to go over what Sezary Syndrome is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could be. First, it is important to note that Sezary Syndrome stems from those who are afflicted with Cutaneous T-Cell Lymphoma [CTCL]. This is a rare type of cancer that affects the skin of a patient. The cause of it is because of afflicted T-cells that become cancerous. This type of cancer primarily affects the skin, but can spread to the blood through the lymph nodes in some circumstances as well. CTCL is the most common type of T-cell Lymphoma and then this is broken down into two types specifically, which are:

  • Sezary Syndrome
  • Mycosis Fungoides

Sezary syndrome is a rare-advanced type of CTCL where cancerous T-lymphocytes have an impact on the skin and blood of a person. These patients have a huge change in skin color because of it, but an even bigger issue is a painful, itchy rash. Plus, there can also be instances of recurrent skin infections for these patients as well. Some symptoms that these patients with Sezary Syndrome can have, are as follows:

  • Swollen lymph nodes
  • Fatigue
  • Enlarged liver
  • Inability to tolerate cold

The global Sezary Syndrome market is expected to reach $2.34 billion by 2037. A major problem with these patients is that despite some current treatment options available for them, they still suffer from recurrent disease. Thus, the goal of Innate Pharma is to deploy the use of lacutamab as a monotherapy to treat them. But how can this drug be effective in targeting these patients? It is because it has been developed as an anti-KIR3DL2 antibody. This is a crucial point, because KIR3DL2 [killer immunoglobulin-like receptor] is reported in more than 85% of Sezary Syndrome cases.

In order to see if lacutamab alone as a monotherapy is able to target these patients with relapsed/refractory Sezary Syndrome, it completed the phase 2 open-label TELLOMAK study. This mid-stage trial intends to recruit up to a total of 170 patients. However, the key thing to note is that it has been established to have several cohorts, with each cohort targeting a different T-cell lymphoma patient population. In the case of targeting patients with r/r SS, this was established in Cohort 1, where a total of 56 intent-to-treat [ITT] patients with Sezary Syndrome received monotherapy lacutamab. It was finally revealed that the patients who took this drug alone were able to achieve a confirmed Objective Response Rate [ORR] of 37.5%. There were a total of 2 complete responses [CRs] out of the bunch of these patients, and a median time to response of 2.8 months. This data is quite impressive when you think about it. That's because these were patients who had already received and failed on two prior systemic therapies, including that of already taking POTELIGEO [mogamulizumab]. The promising thing about this positive data released in targeting these patients with r/r SS is that it sets up two other catalysts for investors to keep an eye on. The first catalyst of which is the use of this drug to treat another form of Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma [NHL], which is Peripheral T-Cell Lymphoma [PTCL]. That is, data from a phase 2 study targeting this patient population is expected to be released in 2025. Another potential catalyst that may or may not come about would be a collaboration agreement between Innate and another pharmaceutical company. The reason why is because it already noted that to further progress the use of lacutamab into a phase 3 study targeting patients with CTCL, it is actively seeking a partner willing to fund it. Thus, if this happens, then this is one key event that could boost shareholder value.

Financials

According to the 6-K SEC Filing, Innate Pharma had a cash position of $106.4 million as of June 30th of 2024. With this cash on hand, the company believes that it has enough to fund its operations towards the end of 2025. In an updated corporate presentation January of 2025 on the company's website, it notes that it had $100.5 million as of September 30th of 2024. Thus, the company is continuing to burn through cash, and it is highly likely that it may need to raise funds in the next several months. The only way that it might be able to avoid having to raise cash is if it can do what I highlighted above, which is to establish a collaboration agreement for the lacutamab program for the treatment of patients with CTCL. The company burns roughly $40.29 million in cash in the half-part of the year. That is, this was the total operating expenses noted in the 1st half of 2024 financial report of the 6-K SEC Filing. On March 27th of 2025, it is going to post a publication of 2024 financial statements and then on May 13th of 2025, it is going to post revenue and cash position for Q1 of 2025.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Innate Pharma. The first risk would be in terms of the ongoing development of lacutamab, which has already been explored in the open-label phase 2 TELLOMAK study. The goal of the company is not to only initiate a phase 3 study targeting CTCL patients, but to establish a collaboration agreement to obtain funding for it. The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will be able to find a partner willing to fund this phase 3 study using lacutamab to target such patients for starters. Even if such a late-stage clinical study is ultimately initiated [with or without a partner in place], there is no assurance that the final primary endpoint is going to be established with statistical significance.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity of lacutamab that might be possible. It is expected that Innate will release data from a phase 2 study using this drug as a monotherapy to treat patients with PTCL in 2025. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the trial data to be released from this mid-stage study is going to be positive, nor that it will be good enough to move towards the initiation of a phase 3 study thereafter.

A third risk to consider would be in terms of the collaboration agreements that Innate Pharma has already been able to establish. It established an agreement with AstraZeneca (AZN) to develop anti-CD39 antibody IPH5201 as a neoadjuvant for the treatment of patients with early-stage, operable non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC]. This partnership has allowed Innate to obtain $60 million in cash and the ability to earn up to $885 million in milestone payments. Then, it also established an agreement with Sanofi (SNY) to develop the use of three molecule candidates, including one which has not yet been disclosed and remains as an option for the big pharma. Should this entire program work out completely, then Innate is entitled to receive up to $1.82 billion in milestone payments plus royalties on net sales for any products marketed. The risk here is that these milestone payments are contingent upon success being established in the clinic. There is no assurance that these product candidates are going to pass all stages of clinical studies, nor be approved for marketing either. If this happens, not only would the stock price of the Innate decline because of it, but it would also not be eligible to earn these massive milestone payments.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. As I noted above in the financial section of this article, it only had a cash position of $100.5 million. The expected time frame on when it believes its cash runway would extend to would be by the end of 2025. Thus, the risk here is that the company is likely going to need to dilute or raise funds using another financial tool in the next several months. The only way it could avoid having to dilute shareholders would be if it can establish a partnership or collaboration agreement with a big pharma for lacutamab or another clinical candidate from its pipeline.

Conclusion

Innate Pharma has done well to continue to develop its pipeline. It has been able to complete the open-label phase 2 TELLOMAK study using lacutamab for the treatment of patients with relapsed/refractory Sezary Syndrome [r/r SS]. Not only was such a mid-stage trial completed, but the company noted that the drug itself as a monotherapy was able to help patients achieve a confirmed Objective Response Rate [ORR] of 37.5%. The promising aspect of this biotech is that it sets up two catalysts that investors can look forward to. This would be the expected data from the phase 2 study using lacutamab to target patients with PTCL and then also the possibility of initiating a phase 3 study with a partner to target CTCL patients. With positive data established using lacutamab for the treatment of patients with r/r Sezary Syndrome in a phase 2 open-label study, plus the ability to develop next-generation ADCs like IPH4502 in targeting low-medium expressing Nectin-4 solid tumors [Especially those resistant to PADCEV], I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: BXSL: World-Class BDC With A Great Yield
data.description:
Article Thesis

Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (NYSE:BXSL) is a high-quality BDC that offers an excellent dividend yield of 9% and that has recently been delivering excellent returns. Despite this, the BDC is not trading at an expensive valuation at all. Add a low-risk portfolio, and BXSL continues to look attractive.

Past Coverage

I have written about Blackstone Secured Lending Fund in the past here on Seeking Alpha, most recently in October 2024 in this bullish article. Since then, the company has delivered an excellent return of 19%, easily outpacing the broad market's return of 5% over the same time frame. With several months having passed since my last article on BXSL, and with the company releasing its next quarterly earnings results soon - at the end of February - it's time to take another look at Blackstone Secured Lending and what investors can expect from the company going forward.

BXSL: A High-Quality BDC

There are many business development companies investors can pick from, but they aren't created equal. Some of them have better track records while others have performed worse - management's ability when it comes to controlling risks and finding lucrative investment options plays a huge role. Business risks differ a lot between different BDCs as well, with factors such as diversification and the asset mix being highly important, I believe.

Based on these and some other factors, Blackstone Secured Lending looks like one of the best BDCs from a quality perspective:

- The company almost exclusively invests in first-lien secured debt, which is one of the safest assets available for investment due to being so high on the capital stack. Relative to BDCs with higher second-lien debt allocations, mezzanine allocations, or equity allocations, BXSL has a very low-risk business model. At the end of the most recent quarter, BXSL's first-lien secured debt investments made up almost 99% of its total investments. At the same time, the average loan-to-value ratio across BXSL's portfolio was relatively low, at 47%, meaning its average portfolio company would have to suffer from hefty losses before BXSL would be impacted.

- Blackstone Secured Lending generates compelling business growth likely thanks to the help of the excellent Blackstone Inc. (BX) brand and its huge network that makes it easy for Blackstone Secured Lending to source deals. Blackstone Secured Lending saw its total investments climb from $9.4 billion at the end of the previous year's third quarter to $11.3 billion at the end of 2024's Q3, which makes for a growth rate of around 20% in a single year. This strong growth in BXSL's investments allowed the company to grow its net investment income by 16% during the third quarter (the Q4 report is not out yet), and we can expect that investment spending will remain high throughout 2025 and beyond, which should allow BXSL to keep its net investment income growing as well. These investments are partially funded via the issuance of new shares, thus the per-share growth rate isn't quite as good, but BXSL is focused on not only growing on a company-wide basis but also on a per-share basis. Thus, dilution is not too much of a problem. Net asset value per share, for example, has risen in recent years despite the fact that BXSL's share count has risen.

- Blackstone Secured Lending has an excellent total return track record, as we can see in the following chart:

Since its IPO in late 2021, Blackstone Secured Lending has delivered an excellent return of 71%, which is one of the best returns in the BDC universe. Among the major BDCs, only Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) has outperformed BXSL, while FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), Ares Capital (ARCC), the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), and Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) have trailed BXSL's returns - although all of these delivered attractive returns as well in that time frame.

Macro Picture: Higher For Longer

The Federal Reserve had to hike up interest rates in recent years in order to combat inflation, which was running at a multi-decade high. Many investors believed that this would only be a short-term thing, but we know now that inflation has been way more persistent than previously thought. Inflation has not come down to 2% yet, and as of the most recent data, inflation has actually reaccelerated in January. It thus seems unlikely that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates substantially this year - they may forego any interest rate reductions and might actually increase rates, according to some forecasts. Whether the Federal Reserve increases rates slightly this year, keeps them stable, or lowers them slightly this year, it looks like we will have elevated interest rates for the foreseeable future - I don't think we will get back to pandemic-level ultra-low interest rates any time soon.

For a lender like Blackstone Secured Lending, this is good news: Many of BXSL's loans have interest rates that are tied to short-term rates, thus if short-term rates remain elevated, that means that the yields on BXSL's loans will remain elevated as well. I doubt that declining interest rates will be a meaningful headwind in the foreseeable future. Thus, the company should benefit from its heavy investments to a significant degree - the company's loan book growth will not be eaten up by declining rates in a higher-for-longer environment.

The new administration in the US is also focused on reducing regulation in order to allow for better economic growth, which could be positive for BXSL and its peers. Less regulation could reduce operating expenses and if economic growth picks up, even more investment opportunities could arise, which could further fuel BXSL's investment spending.

BXSL: Strong Yield, Low Risk

Blackstone Secured Lending offers a dividend yield of 9% at current prices, based on its regular dividend of $0.77 per share per quarter. The company has made special dividend payments at times in the past, but not in the very recent past. Thus, I think that it's prudent to assume that the regular dividend is what investors will be receiving - special dividends would be a positive surprise, but I would not expect them.

A 9% dividend yield makes for a very strong total return tailwind - no share price appreciation is needed for investors to get a high-single-digit total return, and with a share price increase of just 3% per year, investors could be looking at a hefty 12% annual return. With its heavy investment spending and tailwinds from interest rates remaining higher for longer, I think that there's a good chance that BXSL will grow its net asset value per share at a reasonable pace - assuming its book value multiple does not change, that could make for some share price appreciation on top of the great dividend yield.

No investment is risk-free, but Blackstone Secured Lending Fund seems like a relatively low-risk investment: The focus on the most secure type of debt, the rather low loan-to-value ratio across its investments, the strong diversification across hundreds of portfolio companies, and management's expertise all help reduce risks. During a deep recession, it's likely that some of BXSL's portfolio companies would run into trouble, but I do not think that a deep recession in the US is very likely - and if one should occur, most stocks would be impacted.

All in all, Blackstone Secured Lending is a high-quality, high-yield business development company with a great track record and strong brand. While shares were even cheaper in the past, I do not believe that a going-in yield of 9% makes for a demanding valuation at all, and the earnings multiple of around 10 is not expensive, either. I'm happy to remain long with this dividend machine.

data.title: Hard To Imagine A Retirement Portfolio Without These 3 Picks
data.description:
I think that most of you would agree with me that retirement investing is primarily about defense, durability and ensuring that the risk of outliving the saved asset base is really taken out of the equation.

The other side of retirement investing (once the risks are managed) is to enhance the return side. And this is where things start to become tricky as investors have to strike a balance between acceptable risk and acceptable return.

For example, if one invested in only bank deposits, investment-grade bonds, and, say, gold, the returns would be minimal. Theoretically, it could be fine to do so provided that the accumulated capital is very large so that the return is almost irrelevant as long as the wealth is preserved on an inflation-adjusted basis.

This is not my case.

In my case, I am still in my young thirties, trying to compound the necessary amount of passive income snowball by using as minimal capital as possible, while keeping the risk-check-boxes filled.

There are three broad risk parameters that I try to avoid:

  1. Dividend cut.
  2. Loss of dividend purchasing power.
  3. Permanent impairment of capital.

To tick these boxes, it usually boils down to investment-grade businesses with conservative dividend coverage levels that are supported by defensive and stable cash-flowing business models.

I will present now 2 picks that form the core of my retirement income portfolio.

#1 - Brookfield Infrastructure Partners

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP)(BIP.UN:CA) is an infrastructure play, which is automatically introduces several attractive characteristics to the strategy that I outlined above in the intro section.

The infrastructure as such is a defensive asset, which helps preserve the purchasing power of the contracted cash flows. Most of the contracts between the private developer (like BIP) and the public authorities include CPI-clauses or fixed revenue bumps.

In the figure below, we can nicely see how the overall business model works.

There is a CPI component, a demand or use-based component, and, importantly, the reinvested growth component. Together, these three pieces should secure a long-term growth of around 7%.

If we look deeper into the BIP's fundamentals, we will see that there is a lot of firepower to accommodate the growth sustainably.

The FFO payout is ~67%, which means that a notable amount of cash is left inside the business to fund new growth projects.

The balance sheet is at an investment-grade level with a well-laddered debt maturity profile, which helps keep both the interest rate and refinancing risk low.

Plus, one of BIP's tactical funding sources is the asset rotation program, where it sells full and/or partial stakes of completed projects to crystallize value and access fresh liquidity.

All of these elements are clearly supportive of capturing incremental growth (on top of the organic levers). For instance, this will come in handy to cover the data center play, which in 2024 resulted in an FFO growth of 21%.

Finally, we have to also appreciate the fact that BIP has managed to increase its dividend for 16 consecutive years with the most recent increase at 6%.

BIP at a current yield of 5.1% looks very attractive.

#2 - Main Street Capital

Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) is another passive income machine, that has delivered consistent dividends throughout the periods such as the GFC, COVID-19, and interest rate surge shock.

For some MAIN might seem a counterintuitive retirement pick because of its business model that is associated with private credit lending. Private credit tends to involve high risks as the lending is made towards relatively small businesses that cannot easily access capital from traditional banks.

However, MAIN is different.

The reason it has been able to distribute stable and gradually growing dividends is because of its prudent capital allocation approach.

Here are the main points.

First, MAIN has not only a traditional BDC investment portfolio, but also an external asset management arm, which offers a truly passive income source that has a way lower risk profile than general BDC lending.

Second, MAIN lends/invests in high-quality businesses (on a first-lien basis) that have already well-established cash flow profiles. For instance, it has one of the strongest portfolio credit statistics in the industry. The weighted average senior leverage is at 2.8x EBITDA, and senior interest coverage to EBITDA is at 2.4x.

Third, MAIN holds on average 39% of equity ownership in all LMM portfolio companies, where more than 65% of these equity investments produce already consistent dividends. In practice, it creates an opportunity for MAIN to access equity-like returns from investment monetization.

Finally, MAIN has one of the lowest debt levels in the sector. The debt-to-equity stands at 0.81x, which is a level that can easily support the portfolio growth in a prudent manner without increasing the overall financial risk.

At a current dividend yield of 6.8% and the aforementioned fundamental, MAIN is a clear long-term income buy.

#3 - Realty Income

Realty Income Corporation (O) is my favorite REIT, which has one of the strongest fundamentals in the sector. It has also had one of the weakest total return results since the moment when the Fed started to hike the interest rates. While BIP and MAIN have both seen their share prices go up over the past several quarters, O still remains in a relatively depressed territory, which makes it an interesting tactical opportunity as well.

When it comes to defense, it is difficult to find a more protected REIT. For example, it is one of the few REITs that carry an upper investment grade balance sheet, which in combination with a market cap of ~$48 billion allows O to keep the WACC as low as possible. In the REIT business, which is primarily about capturing spreads between cap rates and WACC, having a low WACC is strategically (from the long-term value perspective) important.

An argument that I often hear from the Realty Income bears is that O has a weak portfolio that is subject to a very minimal growth rate.

Here is the thing.

First, it is not only about cap rates and the income side, but also about the cost of capital, where O has a clear advantage.

Second, I think many investors have not yet appreciated this, but O is slowly but surely diversifying away from the traditional net-lease retail model. About 20% of the NOI now stems from the high multiple (growth) property segment.

Third, as of Q3, 2024, O had a weighted average lease maturity of just above 10 years, which nicely mitigates the vacancy risk, and, instead, offers a secured organic growth trajectory on top of the incremental FFO that comes from new growth projects.

Finally, speaking of new growth projects or property acquisitions, we have to definitely factor in the fact that ~25% of the annual FFO generation is retained in O's books. This is a power source for funding additional growth without being fully dependent on the balance sheet, which is, obviously, a clear advantage in terms of risk mitigation and sustainable growth.

At a dividend yield of 5.7%, an upper investment grade balance sheet, and a dividend aristocrat status, it would be hard to imagine not having O in my retirement income portfolio.

The bottom line

In my opinion, retirement investing is about keeping the risks low first, and then only thinking about how to enhance the income profile. Having said that, this does not necessarily mean that we have to assume only inflation-level returns. In fact, by being selective and putting the work in (due diligence, fundamental analysis), we can find many picks that offer attractive risk-to-reward positions.

In this article, I have presented three picks, which produce durable and enticing dividends - all of which could be easily included in the retirement income portfolios without assuming unacceptable risks.
